source: Yasmin Khan 'The Great Partition' (2007); Census of India 1951
note: Most consequential demographic violence in 20th-century South Asia. The range reflects deep methodological disputes: British records are incomplete, communal militias operated across Punjab and Bengal without documentation, and post-independence Pakistani/Indian governments had political reasons to undercount. Most historians now use 200,000–500,000 as a working estimate; higher figures (up to 2 million) appear in some community accounts.

source: Amartya Sen 'Poverty and Famines' (1981); UK War Cabinet records; Oxford researchers (2022 study)
note: Amartya Sen's foundational analysis identified this as a man-made famine caused by export of food, inflationary speculation, and war policy — not weather. Madhusree Mukerjee (2010) documented Churchill's War Cabinet decisions blocking food imports. UK government has never apologized. Toll of 2–3 million is widely accepted among historians; some estimates reach 4 million.

source: British East India Company records; W.W. Hunter 'Annals of Rural Bengal' (1868)
note: Occurred shortly after the British East India Company seized diwani (revenue collection rights) from Bengal. Company doubled land revenue even during famine conditions. One-third of Bengal's population is the traditional estimate, though modern demographic historians consider this a maximum figure. Widely seen as the first major indictment of Company rule in South Asia.

killed: 800,000+ (total, including violence, famine, and epidemic in aftermath)
injured: Unknown — widespread displacement across north-central India
source: William Dalrymple 'The Last Mughal' (2006); Rudolf Muir 'Meerut Expedition'; British Parliamentary records
note: British reprisals included mass executions, village burnings, and deliberate starvation. Indian casualties are poorly documented by colonial records. British soldier and civilian casualties: ~6,000 killed. The post-1857 famine and epidemics compounded deaths. Modern historians acknowledge the scale of collective punishment meted to rebel regions, particularly Awadh, Bundelkhand, and Delhi.

source: Hunter Commission Report (1920); Indian National Congress investigation (1919)
note: General Reginald Dyer ordered 90 soldiers to fire 1,650 rounds into a crowd of ~20,000 unarmed civilians (Baisakhi gathering) at Jallianwala Bagh, Amritsar, on April 13, 1919. The British figure (379) is widely regarded as an undercount. India's centenary commemoration (2019) prompted UK PM Theresa May's 'deep regret' — but no formal apology. Site is now a national memorial.

killed: 1,383 Indian soldiers; ~722 Chinese (official PLA figures disputed)
injured: ~1,047 Indian soldiers wounded; 1,696 Indian soldiers captured
source: Indian Ministry of Defence; Henderson-Brooks-Bhagat Report (classified); PLA records
note: China launched a large-scale offensive along the Himalayan border in October–November 1962, capturing Aksai Chin and parts of NEFA (Arunachal Pradesh). India suffered a decisive military defeat under ill-prepared conditions. Nehru's 'Hindi-Chini bhai-bhai' policy collapsed; the Henderson-Brooks Report (1962) documenting India's failures remains classified. The conflict fundamentally shaped India's defence posture and distrust of China.

source: Indian Ministry of Defence; Shaukat Riza 'The Pakistan Army 1966-71'
note: Triggered by Pakistani Operation Gibraltar (infiltration in Kashmir) and Indian counter-offensive into Punjab. The 22-day war ended in UN-brokered ceasefire. No territorial changes resulted. India claimed a moral victory after repelling Pakistan's offensive; PM Lal Bahadur Shastri's leadership galvanized national resolve. He died in Tashkent the day after signing the peace agreement (January 11, 1966).

injured: ~9,851 Indian soldiers wounded; 10 million refugees fled to India
source: Indian Armed Forces records; Hamoodur Rahman Commission (Pakistan); Bangladesh Liberation War Museum
note: India intervened militarily on December 3, 1971, after Pakistan launched pre-emptive airstrikes on Indian airfields. Indian forces achieved one of the fastest military victories in history: Pakistan surrendered in 13 days (December 16, 1971), creating Bangladesh. The broader Bengali civilian death toll (Pakistani army atrocities against Bangladesh) ranges from 300,000 (Pakistan's Hamoodur Rahman Commission) to 3 million (Bangladeshi and Indian accounts). Pakistan has never acknowledged the genocide.

injured: Thousands; hundreds of thousands displaced from Delhi
source: Nanavati Commission Report (2005); HRW 'Carnage in Delhi' (1984); Sikh Coalition
note: Following PM Indira Gandhi's assassination by her Sikh bodyguards (October 31, 1984), mobs attacked Sikh communities across India — particularly Delhi — for three days. The Nanavati Commission found Congress leaders incited violence. Multiple convictions followed decades later (2018: Sajjan Kumar convicted, sentenced to life imprisonment). The violence is recognized by historians as a state-sanctioned pogrom. The Indian government has never issued a formal apology.

killed: 1,044 (official Gujarat state count); 2,000+ (NHRC and human rights groups)
source: National Human Rights Commission Report (2002); HRW 'We Have No Orders to Save You' (2002); Gujarat government records
note: Triggered by the Godhra train burning (February 27, 2002) killing 59 Hindu pilgrims (circumstances themselves disputed). The subsequent anti-Muslim violence across Gujarat lasted three months. NHRC found police complicity and 'acts of omission and commission.' Modi was Chief Minister; the SIT found no prosecutable evidence against him personally. Multiple senior officials and VHP/BJP leaders were convicted. The Gulberg Society massacre (69 killed) and Naroda Patiya massacre (97 killed) were among the worst incidents.
